owasp_top10: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
mitre_attack: T1070.001 - Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
T1070.002 - Indicator Removal: Clear Linux or Mac System Logs
T1070.003 - Indicator Removal: Clear Command History
T1562.002 - Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging
T1562.008 - Impair Defenses: Disable Cloud Logs
cwe: CWE-117 - Improper Output Neutralization for Logs

threat.title: SIEM Log Tampering and Deletion for Attack Concealment
threat.description: Attackers systematically delete, tamper with, or disable security logs to hide evidence of unauthorized access, lateral movement, data exfiltration, and malicious activities. By clearing Windows Event Logs, manipulating syslog timestamps, disabling CloudWatch logging, or compromising centralized log aggregation systems, adversaries can operate undetected and evade incident response. This represents a critical repudiation threat where attackers deny their actions by eliminating audit trails.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains initial access via phishing or credential compromise (MITRE T1078)
2. Escalates privileges to local admin or SYSTEM (T1068, T1134)
3. Disables Windows Event Logging or Syslog forwarding to prevent new log generation (T1562.002)
4. Clears existing Windows Event Logs using wevtutil.exe or PowerShell (T1070.001)
5. Performs lateral movement, credential dumping, and data exfiltration undetected
6. Re-enables logging services to avoid suspicion, leaving security teams with incomplete audit trails

Alternative Cloud Scenario:
- Attacker compromises AWS access keys with logs:DeleteLogGroup permissions
- Deletes CloudWatch Log Groups containing evidence of S3 bucket enumeration and data access
- Modifies CloudTrail settings to stop logging or exclude sensitive API calls
- Exfiltrates data from S3 buckets with no forensic evidence remaining
prerequisites: Attacker has obtained local administrator or SYSTEM privileges on Windows
Attacker has root/sudo access on Linux systems
Attacker has compromised cloud credentials with logs:Delete* or logging.write permissions
Centralized logging (SIEM forwarding) is not configured or is bypassable
Log files lack immutability protection (write-once, append-only)
No tamper-evident logging or cryptographic log sealing is in place

cve_references.description: EternalBlue SMB exploit used by NotPetya/WannaCry ransomware to spread and clear event logs
Zerologon - Netlogon privilege escalation used by APT groups to clear Domain Controller logs
Log4Shell - Exploited to disable logging infrastructure and hide post-exploitation activities

real_world_breaches.impact: Global supply chain disruption, $10 billion in damages; attackers cleared Windows Event Logs to hide lateral movement via EternalBlue and credential theft
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Malware deliberately executed 'wevtutil.exe cl System' and 'wevtutil.exe cl Security' to erase forensic evidence before encryption

real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple government and critical infrastructure targets; attackers cleared Security and System event logs on domain controllers after credential dumping with Mimikatz
real_world_breaches.root_cause: PowerShell command 'Clear-EventLog -LogName Security,System,Application' executed after obtaining SYSTEM privileges

real_world_breaches.impact: 18,000+ organizations compromised; attackers disabled CloudTrail logging and deleted CloudWatch Logs in AWS environments to hide data exfiltration
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Compromised Orion updates included functionality to disable cloud logging: 'aws cloudtrail stop-logging --name <trail>', 'aws logs delete-log-group --log-group-name <group>'

real_world_breaches.impact: 30,000+ organizations targeted; attackers cleared IIS logs and Windows Event Logs to hide webshell deployment and mailbox access
real_world_breaches.root_cause: PowerShell scripts deleted Exchange logs: 'Remove-Item C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\W3SVC1\* -Force'

bug_bounty_reports.summary: CloudWatch Logs deletion vulnerability allowing attackers to erase audit trails via misconfigured IAM policy
Syslog forwarding bypass allowing local privilege escalation and log tampering on Linux servers

security_research.title: The Art of Anti-Forensics: Log Deletion and Tampering Techniques
security_research.key_finding: 87% of ransomware samples analyzed in 2022 included log deletion functionality; Windows Event Log clearing is the most common anti-forensics technique

security_research.title: MITRE ATT&CK: Indicator Removal on Host (T1070)
security_research.key_finding: 92% of APT groups use log deletion as part of their standard operational procedures

security_research.title: Cloud Log Tampering: AWS CloudTrail and CloudWatch Evasion
security_research.key_finding: Default AWS IAM policies often grant excessive logs:Delete permissions to applications and developers

mitigations.title: AWS S3 Object Lock for Immutable Logging (WORM Compliance Mode)
mitigations.description: Configure AWS S3 with Object Lock in Compliance Mode to create write-once-read-many (WORM) log storage. This prevents deletion or modification of logs even by root AWS accounts, ensuring forensic integrity. Combine with CloudTrail and CloudWatch Logs forwarding for comprehensive audit trails.

tools.name: AWS S3 Object Lock
tools.cost: Standard S3 pricing + $0.0025 per 1,000 Object Lock requests

nist_csf: PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined and documented
DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
PR.DS-6 - Integrity checking mechanisms verify software integrity
validation_steps: Create S3 bucket with object_lock_enabled = true
Configure Compliance Mode with 7-year retention
Test: Attempt to delete log object as root user (should fail with AccessDenied)
Enable CloudTrail log file validation (SHA-256 digest)
Verify CloudWatch Logs export to S3 with retention tags
Apply SCP to prevent cloudtrail:DeleteTrail across organization

mitigations.title: Azure Blob Immutability with Time-Based Retention Policies
mitigations.description: Use Azure Blob Storage with immutability policies and legal hold to create tamper-proof audit logs. Combined with Azure Monitor and Diagnostic Settings, this ensures complete forensic trails for compliance (SOC2, ISO27001, HIPAA).

tools.name: Azure Blob Storage Immutability
tools.cost: $0.018 per GB/month (Hot tier) + standard transaction costs

tools.name: Azure Monitor
tools.cost: $2.76 per GB ingested + $0.12 per GB retention beyond 31 days

validation_steps: Create storage account with versioning and change feed enabled
Apply immutability policy with 7-year retention
Lock immutability policy using Azure CLI
Test: Attempt to delete blob (should fail with ImmutabilityPolicyError)
Configure Azure Monitor Diagnostic Settings
Apply Azure Policy to enforce immutability across subscription

mitigations.title: Write-Once Syslog Forwarding with TLS and Cryptographic Sealing
mitigations.description: Configure rsyslog or syslog-ng to immediately forward all logs to a remote, append-only SIEM server over TLS. Implement cryptographic log sealing (RFC 5848) to detect tampering attempts. Even if local logs are deleted, centralized copies remain intact.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Production-ready rsyslog configuration for immutable centralized logging

# Install rsyslog-gnutls for TLS support
apt-get update && apt-get install -y rsyslog rsyslog-gnutls

# Generate TLS certificates (use proper CA in production)
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout /etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-key.pem \
  -out /etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-cert.pem -days 365 -nodes \
  -subj "/C=US/ST=CA/L=SF/O=Company/CN=siem.company.com"

chmod 600 /etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-key.pem
chmod 644 /etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-cert.pem

# Configure rsyslog client to forward all logs over TLS
cat > /etc/rsyslog.d/10-remote-siem.conf << 'EOF'
# Load TLS driver
module(load="imuxsock") # Local syslog
module(load="imklog")   # Kernel logs

# Configure TLS for remote transmission
global(
  DefaultNetstreamDriver="gtls"
  DefaultNetstreamDriverCAFile="/etc/rsyslog.d/ca-cert.pem"
  DefaultNetstreamDriverCertFile="/etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-cert.pem"
  DefaultNetstreamDriverKeyFile="/etc/rsyslog.d/rsyslog-key.pem"
)

# Action queue for reliability (disk-assisted memory queue)
action(
  type="omfwd"
  target="siem.company.com"
  port="6514"
  protocol="tcp"
  StreamDriver="gtls"
  StreamDriverMode="1"              # TLS encryption
  StreamDriverAuthMode="x509/name"
  StreamDriverPermittedPeers="siem.company.com"
  
  # Queue configuration for resilience
  queue.type="LinkedList"           # In-memory queue
  queue.size="50000"                # Queue up to 50k messages
  queue.dequeueBatchSize="1000"
  queue.workerThreads="4"
  
  # Disk-assisted queue (spills to disk if memory full)
  queue.filename="remote-siem-queue"
  queue.maxDiskSpace="2g"           # Max 2GB disk queue
  queue.saveOnShutdown="on"
  queue.highWatermark="40000"
  queue.lowWatermark="10000"
  
  # Retry logic
  action.resumeRetryCount="-1"      # Infinite retries
  action.resumeInterval="30"        # Retry every 30 seconds
)

# Sign logs with cryptographic hash (RFC 5848 - prevents tampering)
module(load="omsign")
action(
  type="omsign"
  cry.provider="ksi_ls12"           # Use KSI signature provider
  sig.hashFunction="SHA2-256"
  key="/etc/rsyslog.d/signing-key.pem"
)

# Forward ALL facility/priority combinations
*.*  action(type="omfwd" Target="siem.company.com" Port="6514" Protocol="tcp")
EOF

# Configure rsyslog server (SIEM collector) - append-only storage
cat > /etc/rsyslog.d/20-siem-server.conf << 'EOF'
# SIEM Server Configuration (runs on siem.company.com)

# Load TLS input module
module(
  load="imtcp"
  StreamDriver.Name="gtls"
  StreamDriver.Mode="1"
  StreamDriver.AuthMode="x509/name"
  PermittedPeer=["*.company.com"]
)

input(
  type="imtcp"
  port="6514"
  StreamDriver.Name="gtls"
  StreamDriver.Mode="1"
  StreamDriver.AuthMode="x509/name"
)

# Template for structured logging (includes hostname, timestamp, process)
template(
  name="RemoteLogFormat"
  type="string"
  string="%TIMESTAMP:::date-rfc3339% %HOSTNAME% %syslogtag%%msg:::sp-if-no-1st-sp%%msg:::drop-last-lf%\n"
)

# Write logs to append-only directory (set immutable attribute)
action(
  type="omfile"
  file="/var/log/siem/clients/%HOSTNAME%/%$YEAR%-%$MONTH%-%$DAY%.log"
  template="RemoteLogFormat"
  fileCreateMode="0440"    # Read-only after creation
  dirCreateMode="0550"     # Read-execute only
  FileOwner="syslog"
  FileGroup="adm"
)
EOF

# Set immutable attribute on log directory (requires root to remove)
# This prevents deletion even by root until attribute is cleared
mkdir -p /var/log/siem/clients
chattr +a /var/log/siem/clients  # Append-only mode

# Alternative: Use filesystem ACLs to prevent deletion
setfacl -R -m u::rwx,g::rx,o::--- /var/log/siem/clients
setfacl -R -d -m u::rwx,g::rx,o::--- /var/log/siem/clients

# Restart rsyslog
systemctl restart rsyslog
systemctl status rsyslog

# Verify TLS connection
logger -t test "Test message to SIEM"
tail -f /var/log/syslog

# Monitor rsyslog queue status
rsyslogd -N1  # Syntax check

# Create systemd service to monitor log forwarding health
cat > /etc/systemd/system/rsyslog-monitor.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Rsyslog Forwarding Health Monitor
After=rsyslog.service

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/check-rsyslog-forwarding.sh

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Health check script
cat > /usr/local/bin/check-rsyslog-forwarding.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# Alert if rsyslog queue is backing up (indicates SIEM connectivity issues)

QUEUE_SIZE=$(rsyslogd -d 2>&1 | grep -oP 'queue.size=\K[0-9]+')
if [ "$QUEUE_SIZE" -gt 40000 ]; then
  echo "WARNING: Rsyslog queue size is $QUEUE_SIZE (>40k threshold)"
  # Send alert to monitoring system
  curl -X POST https://monitoring.company.com/api/alerts \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"severity":"high","message":"Rsyslog queue backup detected"}'
fi
EOF

chmod +x /usr/local/bin/check-rsyslog-forwarding.sh

# Schedule health check every 5 minutes
echo "*/5 * * * * /usr/local/bin/check-rsyslog-forwarding.sh" | crontab -

echo "Rsyslog configuration complete. Logs are now forwarded to SIEM over TLS."

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
validation_steps: Install rsyslog-gnutls and configure TLS certificates
Configure remote forwarding with TLS to SIEM server
Set chattr +a on /var/log/siem/clients (append-only)
Test: Attempt to delete logs (should fail with Operation not permitted)
Generate test logs: logger -t test 'Immutable log test'
Verify logs appear on SIEM server: tail -f /var/log/siem/clients/<hostname>/
Test tamper detection by modifying a log file (signature verification should fail)

mitigations.title: Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) with Protected Event Log Service
mitigations.description: Configure Windows Event Forwarding to centralize Security and System event logs to a hardened Windows Event Collector (WEC) server. Use SACL auditing on Event Log service to detect tampering attempts. Implement Group Policy to prevent wevtutil.exe and Clear-EventLog execution.

code_example.code:
# Production-ready Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) Configuration
# Run on Domain Controller or management server

# Step 1: Configure Windows Event Collector (WEC) Server
# Enable Windows Remote Management
Enable-PSRemoting -Force
winrm quickconfig -q

# Configure WinRM to allow Event Forwarding
winrm set winrm/config/client '@{TrustedHosts="*"}'

# Create Event Subscriptions for Security, System, Application logs
$subscriptionXML = @'
<Subscription xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/2006/03/windows/events/subscription">
  <SubscriptionId>Security-Critical-Events</SubscriptionId>
  <SubscriptionType>SourceInitiated</SubscriptionType>
  <Description>Forward all Security log events from domain computers</Description>
  <Enabled>true</Enabled>
  <Uri>http://schemas.microsoft.com/wbem/wsman/1/windows/EventLog</Uri>
  
  <ConfigurationMode>Custom</ConfigurationMode>
  <Delivery Mode="Push">
    <Batching>
      <MaxLatencyTime>30000</MaxLatencyTime>  <!-- 30 seconds -->
      <MaxItems>100</MaxItems>
    </Batching>
    <PushSettings>
      <Heartbeat Interval="3600000"/>  <!-- 1 hour -->
    </PushSettings>
  </Delivery>
  
  <Query>
    <![CDATA[
      <QueryList>
        <Query Id="0">
          <Select Path="Security">*[System[(Level=1 or Level=2 or Level=3)]]</Select>
          <Select Path="System">*[System[Provider[@Name='Microsoft-Windows-Eventlog']]]</Select>
          <Select Path="System">*[System[(EventID=104 or EventID=1102)]]</Select>  <!-- Log clearing events -->
        </Query>
      </QueryList>
    ]]>
  </Query>
  
  <ReadExistingEvents>true</ReadExistingEvents>
  <TransportName>HTTP</TransportName>
  <ContentFormat>RenderedText</ContentFormat>
  <Locale Language="en-US"/>
  <LogFile>ForwardedEvents</LogFile>
  <AllowedSourceDomainComputers>O:NSG:NSD:(A;;GA;;;DC)(A;;GA;;;NS)</AllowedSourceDomainComputers>
</Subscription>
'@

# Import subscription
$subscriptionXML | Out-File -FilePath "C:\Temp\security-subscription.xml" -Encoding UTF8
wecutil cs "C:\Temp\security-subscription.xml"

# Verify subscription
wecutil gs Security-Critical-Events

# Step 2: Configure Event Log Size and Retention (prevent local log overflow)
wevtutil sl Security /ms:4294967296      # 4GB Security log
wevtutil sl System /ms:2147483648        # 2GB System log
wevtutil sl Application /ms:2147483648   # 2GB Application log

# Set retention to Overwrite as needed (older events overwritten)
wevtutil sl Security /rt:false
wevtutil sl System /rt:false

# Step 3: Enable Audit Policy for Event Log Service Access
# Detect attempts to clear logs or stop Event Log service
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Audit Policy Change" /success:enable /failure:enable
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Security System Extension" /success:enable /failure:enable

# Step 4: Configure SACL on Event Log Service to detect tampering
# Alert when someone tries to stop Event Log service or modify registry keys

$EventLogServiceACL = @'
reg add "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\EventLog\Security" /v "CustomSD" /t REG_SZ /d "D:(A;;CCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRRC;;;SY)(A;;CCDCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;BA)(A;;CCLCSWLOCRRC;;;AU)S:(AU;FA;CCDCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;WD)" /f
'@

# Translation: SACL alerts on any access by Everyone (WD)
# This generates Event ID 4663 when Event Log registry is accessed

# Step 5: Block wevtutil.exe and Clear-EventLog cmdlet via AppLocker
# Create AppLocker policy to deny wevtutil.exe except for administrators

$appLockerPolicy = @'
<AppLockerPolicy Version="1">
  <RuleCollection Type="Exe" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <!-- Block wevtutil.exe for non-admins -->
    <FilePathRule Id="a61c8b2c-a319-4cd0-9690-d2177cad7b51" Name="Block wevtutil" Description="" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Deny">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="%SYSTEM32%\wevtutil.exe" />
      </Conditions>
      <Exceptions>
        <!-- Allow Domain Admins -->
        <FilePathCondition Path="%SYSTEM32%\wevtutil.exe" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-5-32-544" />
      </Exceptions>
    </FilePathRule>
  </RuleCollection>
</AppLockerPolicy>
'@

$appLockerPolicy | Out-File -FilePath "C:\Temp\applocker-policy.xml" -Encoding UTF8
Set-AppLockerPolicy -XmlPolicy "C:\Temp\applocker-policy.xml" -Merge

# Enable AppLocker service
Set-Service -Name AppIDSvc -StartupType Automatic
Start-Service -Name AppIDSvc

# Step 6: Group Policy to disable PowerShell Clear-EventLog cmdlet
# Create GPO to block Clear-EventLog via Constrained Language Mode

New-GPO -Name "Block-EventLog-Clearing" | New-GPLink -Target "OU=Servers,DC=company,DC=com"

$gpoScript = @'
# Configure PowerShell to run in Constrained Language Mode (prevents Clear-EventLog)
$key = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell"
New-Item -Path $key -Force
Set-ItemProperty -Path $key -Name "ExecutionPolicy" -Value "RemoteSigned"
Set-ItemProperty -Path $key -Name "EnableScripts" -Value 1

# Block dangerous cmdlets
$key = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PowerShell\ExecutionPolicy"
New-Item -Path $key -Force
Set-ItemProperty -Path $key -Name "ShellIds" -Value "Microsoft.PowerShell"
'@

# Step 7: Export ForwardedEvents log to immutable storage every hour
$exportScript = @'
# Export ForwardedEvents log to immutable S3 bucket
$timestamp = Get-Date -Format "yyyyMMdd-HHmmss"
$logFile = "C:\Logs\ForwardedEvents-$timestamp.evtx"

wevtutil epl ForwardedEvents $logFile

# Upload to AWS S3 with Object Lock
aws s3 cp $logFile s3://company-siem-logs-immutable/windows-events/ --storage-class STANDARD_IA

# Verify upload and delete local copy
if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) {
    Remove-Item $logFile -Force
    Write-EventLog -LogName Application -Source "LogExport" -EventId 1000 -Message "ForwardedEvents log exported to S3: $logFile"
}
'@

$exportScript | Out-File -FilePath "C:\Scripts\export-forwarded-events.ps1" -Encoding UTF8

# Schedule task to run every hour
$action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "PowerShell.exe" -Argument "-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Scripts\export-forwarded-events.ps1"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Once -At (Get-Date) -RepetitionInterval (New-TimeSpan -Hours 1)
$principal = New-ScheduledTaskPrincipal -UserId "SYSTEM" -LogonType ServiceAccount
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "Export-ForwardedEvents" -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -Principal $principal

# Step 8: Monitor for Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared)
# Create Windows Event Subscription to alert on log clearing

$alertSubscription = @'
<QueryList>
  <Query Id="0" Path="Security">
    <Select Path="Security">*[System[(EventID=1102)]]</Select>  <!-- Audit log was cleared -->
    <Select Path="System">*[System[(EventID=104)]]</Select>      <!-- System log was cleared -->
  </Query>
</QueryList>
'@

$alertSubscription | Out-File -FilePath "C:\Temp\log-clearing-alert.xml" -Encoding UTF8

# Attach action to send email alert
$taskAction = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "PowerShell.exe" -Argument "-Command Send-MailMessage -To security@company.com -From wec@company.com -Subject 'ALERT: Event Log Cleared' -SmtpServer smtp.company.com"

# Register event-triggered task
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "Alert-Log-Clearing" -Action $taskAction -Trigger (New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtLogOn)

Write-Host "Windows Event Forwarding configuration complete."
Write-Host "Logs are now centralized to WEC server and exported to S3 hourly."
Write-Host "Event ID 1102 (log clearing) will trigger email alerts."

validation_steps: Configure WEC server with wecutil cs security-subscription.xml
Enable WinRM on client computers: winrm quickconfig
Create GPO to enable Event Forwarding: Computer Config > Policies > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Event Forwarding
Verify logs forwarding: wecutil gr Security-Critical-Events
Test: Run Clear-EventLog -LogName Security (should be blocked by AppLocker)
Monitor Event ID 1102 in ForwardedEvents log
Schedule hourly export to S3 using Scheduled Tasks

indicators: Windows Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared) or 104 (System log cleared)
Sudden drop in log volume from specific hosts (log gap analysis)
Execution of wevtutil.exe, Clear-EventLog cmdlet, or rm /var/log/* commands
CloudTrail events: logs:DeleteLogGroup, logs:DeleteLogStream, cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail
Unauthorized access to /var/log/ directories or Event Viewer with SeSecurityPrivilege
Timestamp anomalies (logs created in the past or future)
Failed syslog forwarding or TLS connection drops to SIEM server
Chattr attribute changes on /var/log (removal of +a append-only flag)
S3 DeleteObject API calls against log buckets with Object Lock enabled (should fail)
Azure Blob Storage: ImmutabilityPolicy deletion attempts

log_queries.query:
// Detect Windows Event Log clearing (Event ID 1102)
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 1102  // Audit log was cleared
| extend ClearedBy = SubjectUserName
| extend ClearedFrom = Computer
| project TimeGenerated, ClearedBy, ClearedFrom, Activity, EventData
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Detect AWS CloudTrail/CloudWatch log deletion attempts
AWSCloudTrail
| where EventName in ("DeleteLogGroup", "DeleteLogStream", "StopLogging", "DeleteTrail")
| extend Actor = UserIdentityArn
| extend SourceIP = SourceIpAddress
| project TimeGenerated, EventName, Actor, SourceIP, RequestParameters, ResponseElements
| order by TimeGenerated desc

index=windows EventCode=1102 OR EventCode=104
| stats count by host, EventCode, User
| where count > 0
| eval severity="HIGH"
| table _time, host, EventCode, User, severity

# Alternative: Detect log volume drop (gap analysis)
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security
| bucket _time span=1h
| stats count by _time, host
| streamstats window=2 avg(count) as avg_count by host
| eval drop_percent = ((avg_count - count) / avg_count) * 100
| where drop_percent > 80  # 80% drop in log volume
| table _time, host, count, avg_count, drop_percent

event.action: "file_deletion" AND file.path: /var/log/*
OR process.name: "wevtutil.exe" AND process.args: ("cl" OR "clear-log")
OR event.code: (1102 OR 104)

sequence by host.name with maxspan=5m
  [process where process.name == "wevtutil.exe" and process.args == "cl"]
  [file where event.action == "deletion" and file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*"]

fields @timestamp, eventName, userIdentity.principalId, sourceIPAddress, errorCode
| filter eventName in ["DeleteLogGroup", "DeleteLogStream", "StopLogging", "DeleteTrail", "PutEventSelectors"]
| sort @timestamp desc
| limit 100

index=linux sourcetype=linux_audit type=EXECVE
| search a0="chattr" a1="-a" OR a1="-i"  # Remove append-only or immutable
| rex field=_raw "name=\"(?<target_file>.*?)\""
| where match(target_file, "/var/log/")
| table _time, host, user, a0, a1, a2, target_file
| eval severity="CRITICAL"

// Detect Azure Blob immutability policy deletion attempts
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue in ("MICROSOFT.STORAGE/STORAGEACCOUNTS/BLOBSERVICES/CONTAINERS/IMMUTABILITYPOLICIES/DELETE", 
                                "MICROSOFT.STORAGE/STORAGEACCOUNTS/BLOBSERVICES/CONTAINERS/IMMUTABILITYPOLICIES/EXTEND")
| extend Actor = Caller
| extend ResourceName = Resource
| project TimeGenerated, OperationNameValue, Actor, ResourceName, ActivityStatusValue
| where ActivityStatusValue == "Failed"  # Should always fail if locked
| order by TimeGenerated desc

tools.name: Sysmon Event ID 1
tools.usage: Detect wevtutil.exe, Clear-EventLog.exe, auditpol.exe with suspicious arguments

tools.name: OSQuery
tools.usage: SELECT * FROM file WHERE path LIKE '/var/log/%' AND action = 'DELETED';

tools.name: Velociraptor
tools.usage: Artifact: Windows.EventLogs.Cleared - Detects Event ID 1102 across fleet

compliance: NIST SP 800-53 (AU-9 - Protection of Audit Information)
CIS Critical Security Controls (8.3, 8.5 - Audit log retention)
